svg.text: YEMENITE HEBREW
SCRIPT — YEMENITE MANUSCRIPT TRADITION
YEMENITE SCRIPT STYLE
Yemenite Hebrew: distinct calligraphic tradition, semi-cursive
Yemenite scribal style: influence of Arabic calligraphy visible
Torah scrolls in Yemen: written in Yemenite scribal hand (distinct from Ashkenazi)
YEMENITE VOWEL POINTING
Yemenite Jews preserve Babylonian vocalization system (not Tiberian)
Babylonian system: vowels written above consonants (vs Tiberian below)
Some Yemenite manuscripts: both systems (Babylonian over, Tiberian under)
Tiberian system (Masoretes) became standard; Yemenite preserves older form
YEMENITE TORAH READING
Yemenite Jews read Torah with verse-by-verse Aramaic translation
(Targum Onkelos): ancient practice preserved only in Yemen
Each verse: Hebrew, then Aramaic translation, then next verse
YEMENITE SCRIPT FEATURES
Letters: more angular than Ashkenazi, rounder than Sephardic
Aleph and ayin: distinct forms from other traditions
ARABIC · SECONDARY LANGUAGE
YEMENI ARABIC
Yemen: Arabic-speaking country surrounding Jewish community
Yemeni Arabic: among most conservative Arabic dialects
Preserves /q/ as stop (many dialects: /q/ → glottal stop or /g/)
Preserves interdental /θ/ (th) and /ð/ (dh) lost in most dialects
JUDEO-YEMENI ARABIC
Yemenite Jews spoke Judeo-Yemeni Arabic in daily life for 2000 years
Hebrew script used to write Yemeni Arabic: Judeo-Arabic tradition
Yemenite Jewish women: often knew only Judeo-Arabic, not Hebrew
SCRIPT WORLDS IN CONTACT
Yemenite Jews: read Hebrew script for prayer, Arabic script for commerce
Two abjad traditions: same family, daily interaction, distinct communities
Yemenite Hebrew manuscripts: some Arabic calligraphy influence in letter forms
PLATE 11a ALT · YEMENITE HEBREW / ARABIC · SCRIPT
